coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a near infrared-actuated phthalocyanine-lipid vesicle system for augmented photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Nwahara, Nnamdi
Managa, Muthumuni
Stoffels, Mihlali
Britton, Jonathan
Prinsloo, Earl
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: The efficacy of photodynamic therapy (PDT) is often limited by the poor bio-distributive properties of conventional photosensitizers and the local hypoxic microenvironment that characterises most solid tumours. Herein, a novel in situ oxygenic lipid formulation for photodynamic therapy (PDT) is reported. Such a hybrid was synthesized by adsorbing bimetallic nanozyme, MnO2@PtNPs (NPs = nanoparticles) onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) – zinc (II) phthalocyanine conjugates, followed by liposomal encapsulation, affording it enhanced water solubility. The MnO2@PtNPs, which are is shown to possess excellent catalase-like properties surpassing that of MnO2 or PtNPs alone, serves to catalyze H2O2 to O2, while the zinc (II) phthalocyanine (1) serves to transform the formed oxygen to generate cytotoxic singlet oxygen immediately. We show that by combining each function of the respective building blocks, the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes not only maintains the properties of oxygen supplementation through H2O2 catalysis but also displays cooperative properties for enhanced singlet oxygen production. Consequently, a remarkably improved PDT efficacy was observed for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes in both normoxia and hypoxia. These results demonstrate the potential applicability of such nanozyme constituted 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes for achieving tumour treatment in hypoxic conditions by PDT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Raman spectral analysis of (i) GQDs and (ii) 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, showing characteristic D and G bands as well as peaks attributable to MnO2 (*) signifying successful synthesis and chemisorption of the MnO2@PtNPs on the GQDs surface to form 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. (B) 3D AFM images of GQDs (i) and 1@GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs (ii) with respective height profiles.

XPS survey spectra of (a) MnO2@PtNPs. High-resolution core-level Pt 4f spectra of (b) PtNPs. High-resolution core-level Mn 2p spectra of (c) MnO2@PtNPs, and the core level Pt 4f spectra of (d) MnO2@PtNPs.

XPS survey spectra of (a) 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. High-resolution core-level N1 s spectra of 1-GQDs (b) and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs (c). High-resolution Pt 4f (d) and Mn 2p (e) core-level spectra of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs.

(A) Ex vivo O2 generation abilities of MnO2@PtNPs (i), PtNPs (ii), MnO2 (iii), in the presence of H2O2. H2O2 alone is used as control (iv). (B) ESR spectra of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs in H2O2, wherein 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) acted as the capturing agent of •OH for detection. (C) Cyclic voltammogram of GCE-1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs electrode in sodium acetate buffer (0.10 M, pH 5.0) in the absence (black) and presence (red) of 6.67 mM H2O2. (Insert: Magnified diagram of the GCE-1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs electrode in sodium acetate buffer (0.10 M, pH 5.0) in the presence (red) of 6.67 mM H2O2).

TEM images of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes with corresponding SEM-EDS mapping images showing the presence of C, O, P, Pt, Mn, and Zn. The SEM image for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes is also represented along with its EDS spectrum.

UV−vis spectra of 1-liposome (a) and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposome (b) with a characteristic peak of 1 at around 677 nm. The insert shows the appearance of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposome before (i) and after (ii) liposomal loading. Solvent = phosphate buffer saline (PBS).
Time-dependent cellular uptake of (i) 1-liposomes and (ii) 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes under (A) normoxia and (B) hypoxia for a total of 104 cells.

TOP: In vitro cytostatic effects of liposomal drug formulations under hypoxia without (A, B) and with H2O2 (C, D) (n = 3) against MCF-7 cells after incubation for 24 h (with B,D and without A,C, laser irradiation). 1-liposomes (i) or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (ii). BOTTOM: TEM images for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes treated cells in the (i) dark and (ii) treated cells after photoirradiation. In all cases, the photoirradiation was performed at 680 nm, 0.28 W cm-2, 10 min. Concentration of the drugs = 100 µg/mL in DMEM.

Illustration of the synthesis of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposome showing mechanism of tumour oxygenation. Near-infrared (NIR) irradiation induces singlet oxygen generation, which is responsible for cancer cell death.
Photophysicochemical data of 1 and its conjugates in DMSO.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of a near infrared-actuated phthalocyanine-lipid vesicle system for augmented photodynamic therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficacy of photodynamic therapy (PDT) is often limited by the poor bio-distributive properties of conventional photosensitizers and the local hypoxic microenvironment that characterises most solid tumours. Herein, a novel in situ oxygenic lipid formulation for photodynamic therapy (PDT) is reported. Such a hybrid was synthesized by adsorbing bimetallic nanozyme, MnO2@PtNPs (NPs = nanoparticles) onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs) – zinc (II) phthalocyanine conjugates, followed by liposomal encapsulation, affording it enhanced water solubility. The MnO2@PtNPs, which are is shown to possess excellent catalase-like properties surpassing that of MnO2 or PtNPs alone, serves to catalyze H2O2 to O2, while the zinc (II) phthalocyanine (1) serves to transform the formed oxygen to generate cytotoxic singlet oxygen immediately. We show that by combining each function of the respective building blocks, the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes not only maintains the properties of oxygen supplementation through H2O2 catalysis but also displays cooperative properties for enhanced singlet oxygen production. Consequently, a remarkably improved PDT efficacy was observed for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes in both normoxia and hypoxia. These results demonstrate the potential applicability of such nanozyme constituted 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes for achieving tumour treatment in hypoxic conditions by PDT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article [and/or] its supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing research interest in photodynamic therapy (PDT), a non-invasive therapeutic modality for cancer, has continued as an alternative to more conventional modalities (such as chemotherapy and radiation). PDT’s local light-controllable activation, as well as its tolerance to repeated doses [1–3] , have been largely responsible for such sustained interest. In principle, the photosensitizer (PS), in its excited triplet state, transfers energy to molecular oxygen to produce reactive singlet oxygen (1O2), which is responsible for cancer cell death [4]. Most PSs that have been applied for PDT have been porphyrin derivatives, many of which are hydrophobic. As a result, they easily aggregate, leading to low tumour cell accumulation, and ultimately, poor clinical outcomes. Effective PSs delivery systems can help ensure a high degree of cellular uptake, where rapid PS release can alleviate the problem of the short half-life as well as limited diffusion distance of singlet oxygen [5,6] .

Most tumour cells exist within a local hypoxic microenvironment, characterised by both a reduced oxygen supply and acidulated microenvironments [7,8] . Hypoxia is an essential consideration in any cancer-related therapy, particularly photodynamic therapy (PDT) since oxygen is a limiting factor. As a result, hypoxic cells are largely considered PDT resistant [9]. Thus, overcoming hypoxia by replenishing the tumour oxygen supply is recognized as vital to PDT success [10–12]. To this end, numerous reports have explored strategies to modulate tumour hypoxia. To date, two major pathways have been highlighted to overcome tumour hypoxia: oxygen-independent PDT (Type I PDT) [13,14], and tumour oxygenation [15]. Although promising, oxygen -independent PDT does not alter the tumour microenvironment and as such, issues such as drug-resistant gene expression remain largely unresolved [15]. In contrast, tumour oxygenation has aroused considerable research interest owing to its ability to alter the tumour microenvironment, as well as attenuating resistance [16,17]. Traditional approaches such as extending tumour irradiation with low fluence rates, only work to reduce oxygen depletion without achieving hypoxic relief [17]. Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) therapy has demonstrated some clinical success in tumour oxygenation [18], however, serious side effects such as hyperoxia and barotrauma, have significantly limited its wide adoption [19,20]. More recently, Liu et al. reported on tandem catalysts comprising catalase encapsulated within metal organic framework (MOF), which was able to convert H2O2 to O2 within tumour cells [21]. However, a disadvantage of such systems is that natural enzymes are inherently fragile and suffer from issues such as narrow optimum pH ranges, as well as low operational stability [22,23].

In contrast, nanozymes possess tunable and efficient enzyme-like activity, high stability, and durability [23], and have been shown to mimic various biological enzymes such as horseradish peroxidase [24,25], oxidase [26], and have catalase-like activities [27,28]. Catalase-mimicking nanozymes such as Au, MnO2, and Pt nanoparticles (NPs) have been shown to readily catalyze H2O2 into O2 within cells [29–31], which offers an excellent opportunity to modulate hypoxia via in situ oxygen production from abundant intracellular H2O2 within hypoxic tumours. Moreover, architecting two nanoparticles into bimetallic nanohybrids and nanocomposites systems has resulted in superior catalytic activities that are not achievable when the component nanomaterials are used [32]. We have recently reported on the enhanced catalase-activity realizable by coupling MnO2 with either AuNPs or AgNPs [33]. In this study, we fabricated bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs, and herein demonstrate its superior catalase-mimicking properties over the constituent MnO2 and PtNPs for the first time.

Metallated phthalocyanines (MPc), such as zinc (II) phthalocyanine (ZnPc), have demonstrated improved PDT activity over many conventional photosensitizers owing to their longer wavelength (near infrared) absorption, high singlet oxygen quantum yield, and substantially higher visible light capture [34,35]. In this work, a zinc ion-based phthalocyanine containing one substituent (phenoxy propionic acid) is used. Porphyrins containing propanoic acid (such as the Uroporphyrins) have been employed successfully for PDT [36], hence this substituent is employed in this work. A novel oxygen self-sufficient metallophthalocyanines-nanozyme composite is herein reported by coupling hydrophobic zinc (II) 2-(carboxyethyl-4-phenyl) phthalocyanine. (1) [37], with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with subsequent conjugation to MnO2@PtNPs (1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs). This hybrid was further loaded into 1,2-di-(9Z-octadecenoyl)-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DOPC) liposomes forming 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, affording them enhanced water dispersibility and physiological stability. This multifunctional nanozyme hybrid (1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes) effectively couples the physiological stability of liposomes, the high singlet oxygen-generating ability of the phthalocyanine, as well as their outstanding near infrared (NIR) absorption, with the high catalytic activity of bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs nanozyme for supplementing O2 from H2O2. Notably, in vitro experimental results demonstrated the remarkable PDT efficacy of the employed 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes in both a normoxic and a hypoxic environment, which is a testament to its ability to circumvent insufficient molecular oxygen levels through the decomposition of H2O2, while producing sufficient singlet oxygen to effect photodynamic lethality. The present work, therefore, presents a novel multifunctional nanozyme hybrid to realize improved PDT efficacy through augmented tumour oxygenation.

Materials and equipment items employed in this work are provided in the supporting information. Details of the synthesis of 1-GQDs, MnO2@PtNPs, and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs and encapsulation of 1 and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs into liposomes are presented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of employed conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic representation highlighting the different steps in the formation of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-Liposome is shown in Scheme 1. The successful amide conjugation of 1 to GQDs (Scheme 1, step 1) was confirmed by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Upon conjugation of 1 to GQDs, a carbonyl peak was observed at 1641 cm-1, a shift from 1702 cm-1 for 1 alone. Such shifts confirm the successful amide linkage and hence conjugation of 1 to GQDs. Taking advantage of the abundance of amino groups of PEI-functionalization on GQDs (in 1-GQDs), the as-synthesized MnO2@PtNPs were adsorbed onto GQDs in 1-GQDs (Scheme 1 , step 2). The synthesis of MnO2@PtNPs and subsequent fabrication of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposome (Scheme 1, step 3) were monitored and confirmed using various techniques outlined below.

TOF-SIMS is a sensitive elemental mapping technique that can provide specifics on the nature of the analyzed surface. In this technique, a pulsed beam of primary ions is used to create secondary ions in a sputtering process. Analyzing the produced secondary ions allows the identification of atoms and molecules present on the surface of particles. In this work, ion density maps, or secondary ion images, were acquired over areas of 70 × 70 µm2. TOF-SIMS was applied to characterize the synthesized MnO2@PtNPs. TOF-SIMS secondary ion images give information on the characteristic distributions of elements/compounds. Subsequently, an RGB (red, green, and blue) plot of the MnO2@PtNPs surface was obtained (Fig. S2). The RGB plot of the TOF-SIMS image of MnO2@PtNPs shows the mass locations of Pt in red colour, MnO2 in green, and Mn in blue. As can be seen in Fig. S2, the TOF-SIMS shows the Mn and Pt species within the MnO2@PtNPs and imply a hybrid-type bimetallic structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline phases of the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs were characterized using powder XRD analysis. Fig. S3 shows the diffraction patterns of GQDs (i), 1@GQDs (ii). The XRD pattern of GQDs showed a typical broad diffraction peak around 23°, indexed as (002) of the interlayer aromatic stacking of graphitic lattices [38]. The presence of 1 in the 1@GQDs conjugate did not significantly alter this diffraction pattern, with no distinguishable peaks being observed (Fig. S3 (ii)). The XRD spectra of MnO2 (i), PtNPs (ii), MnO2@PtNPs (iii), and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs (iv) are presented in Fig. S4. The XRD pattern of MnO2 shows the absence of sharp distinguishable peaks but was instead characterised by multiple broad bands. MnO2 is usually associated with weak X-ray diffraction patterns [39]. The XRD spectrum of PtNPs shows the presence of distinct diffraction patterns at 2θ = 39.82, 46.22, 67.62, 81.22, and 85.91, which were assigned to Pt (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), respectively, planes of the face-cantered cubic (fcc) crystal lattice of platinum [40]. Upon addition of MnO2 in the formation of MnO2@PtNPs, the intensity of the crystalline peak attributed to the presence of PtNPs, decreased. The attenuation of the core nanoparticle peak (in this case, PtNPs) has been observed in the XRD pattern of other core-shell structures [41,42]. X-ray diffraction pattern of MnO2@PtNPs (Fig. S4 (iii)) also shows a peak at 60.2°, assigned (532) and attributable to the presence of MnO2, very weak peaks as reported in the literature [39]. Therefore, MnO2@PtNPs show both MnO2 and PtNPs characters indicative of a bimetallic core-shell structure. However, following conjugation to 1-GQDs to form 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, an amorphous pattern was observed, with no distinct crystalline peaks (Fig. S4 (iv)) typical of GQDs [43] and MPc complexes.

Raman spectroscopy was used to access the changes in the graphitic core structure of GQDs post-modification with either 1, or with the assembly of MnO2@PtNPs. Fig. 1A, Raman spectra of GQDs exhibit a typical D-band (1309 cm-1) corresponding to disordered sp3 defects and a G-band (1462 cm-1) relating to the ordered sp2 C−C bond. Slight shifts were observed for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs with D and G bands occurring at 1290 cm-1 and 1597 cm-1, respectively. The ID/IG (integrated intensity) ratios of D to G bands for GQDs alone and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs were calculated to be 0.17 and 1.34, respectively. This observed increase in the ID/IG (from 0.17 for GQDs alone) is indicative of decreases in the fraction of the sp2 domains. Such changes are associated with the introduction of defects to the basal planes and the edges of carbon structures [44], and hence, signify increases in the structural disorder of the GQDs post-modification. Besides, Raman spectroscopy also allows for the identification of metal oxide NPs (owing to their electron-phonon coupling). The Raman spectrum of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs showed peaks at 494 cm-1 and 677 cm-1, which can be attributed to the Mn-O stretching modes, characteristics peaks of MnO2 [45,46], and further supports its successful synthesis.

Fig. 1B shows the AFM images of GQDs and resulting 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. The topographic height of the GQDs presented was 2.05 nm, which was approximated to two to three graphene layers [44]. The surface roughness of the GQDs was calculated to be 1.5 nm. MnO2@PtNPs were then self-assembled onto the GQDs (in 1-GQDs), affording the resultant 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. There is a marked increase in surface roughness to 85 nm for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. Such increases in topographical height serve as evidence for the successful assembly of MnO2@PtNPs onto the GQDs surface. The successful synthesis of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs is further marked by distinct changes in 3D surface features (uneven surfaces with wrinkles and voids), which are absent in GQDs alone (Fig. 1B (ii)). Transmission electron microscopy was used to assess the morphologies of the respective composites. For simplicity, only the TEM micrographs of GQDs, PtNPs, MnO2@PtNPs, and 1@GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, are shown (Fig. S5B). The TEM micrograph of the as-synthesized GQDs shows that they are monodispersed, quasi-spherical structures with an average diameter of 4 nm. Following conjugation to 1 to form 1-GQDs, no significant changes in the size or shape of the GQDs were observed (figure not shown). Fig. S5B shows monodispersed spherical PtNPs with an average size of 2 nm. Upon compositing of the MnO2 and PtNPs to form MnO2@PtNPs, evidence of aggregation and hence size increases were observed. The MnO2@PtNPs had a size average of 5 nm, which was greater than the size of the core PtNPs. The spherical morphology of the MnO2@PtNPs may be indicative of MnO2 adhering to the surface of the PtNPs, maintaining a 3-dimensional morphology. In the TEM image of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, the presence of individual GQDs, or PtNPs could not be distinguished, however, an increase in the size of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs heterostructure (to 9 nm) was observed ( Table 1). The increase in size for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs is most likely due to aggregation within the heterostructure [37].

Fig. S5A shows the UV-Vis spectra of GQDs (i), 1@GQDs (ii), PtNPs (iii), MnO2@PtNPs (iv), and 1@GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs (v). The UV-Vis spectrum of GQDs shows the presence of a strong absorption below 400 nm and peaking at 360 nm, attributable to the n–π* electronic transitions caused by the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the GQDs [47,48].

The absorption feature of 1 is typical of phthalocyanine complexes with a pronounced Q-band absorption located at 674 nm (Table 1) and a more subdued B-band absorbance at around 350 nm. Table 1, Fig. S5A(iii). Upon conjugation to the GQDs, an insignificant shift in the Q-band absorption to 675 nm of 1@GQDs was observed, but there was an increase in the absorption in the B-band region (below 400), which is attributed to the presence of GQDs. The UV-Vis spectrum of MnO2@PtNPs showed no distinct peaks. Instead, a broad band below 400 nm was observed (Fig. S5A (ii)). The Q-band absorption due to the presence of 1 was evident in 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. There were no shifts in the Q-band absorbance of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs compared to 1-GQDs. However, 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs showed enhanced absorbance below 500 nm, attributable to the presence of MnO2@PtNPs within the composite, strongly suggesting the successful synthesis of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs.

Fig. 2 shows the XPS survey spectra of the MnO2@PtNPs, as well as the high-resolution Mn 2p and Pt 4f core-level spectra of the MnO2 and PtNPs within the bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs. The survey spectrum of MnO2@PtNPs show elements present in the sample at 72 eV (Pt 4f), 283 eV (C 1s), 530 eV (O 1s), and 650 eV (Mn 2p). The appearance of the C 1s peak is mainly from residual citrate used as a capping agent in the synthesis of the PtNPs.

The high-resolution core-level XPS spectra of Mn 2p in the MnO2@PtNPs, as well as the Pt 4f of PtNPs (for the latter both alone and in the MnO2@PtNPs), were analyzed to determine the oxidation state of Mn and Pt within the bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs. In the high-resolution spectrum of the Mn 2p core-level region (Fig. 2c), peaks associated with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 were observed at 641.8 and 653.4 eV, respectively, with a spin-orbit coupling of 11.6 eV, which is characteristic of MnO2 in the Mn4+ oxidation state [49]. A satellite peak beside the Mn 2p3/2 peak was also observed. This peak was attributed to the presence of Mn2+ in the form of MnO [49,50] . The Pt 4f spectrum of the bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs contains two main peaks at 74.17 and 70.88 eV with a difference of 3.3 eV, which are characteristic peaks of 4f5/2 and 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 2d), implying the zero-valent nature of Pt. Meanwhile, the Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 peaks in the PtNPs prior to compositing with MnO2 were observed at 74.72 and 71.10 eV, respectively, (Fig. 2b). The Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of the PtNPs without MnO2 were found to be centered at higher binding energies than those in the MnO2@PtNPs. The introduction of MnO2 therefore led to significant shifts to lower binding energies. A peak at 71.86 eV was also observed in the core-level Pt 4f of PtNPs without MnO2 and was assigned to the Pt2+ oxidation state. However, this peak was not observed in the core-level Pt 4f of MnO2@PtNPs. The disappearance of the Pt2+ peak as well as shifts to lower binding energies suggest that there is a strong electronic interaction between Pt and MnO2 in the MnO2@PtNPs [51–53] . Moreover, such shifts have also been associated with synergistic effects, which are associated with enhanced catalytic activity [52]. Further, it is noted that the introduction of the MnO2 layer significantly attenuated the intensity of the PtNPs within the bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs. A similar feature was obtained from XRD analysis. Taken together, based on the results obtained from XRD, TOF-SIMS, and XPS, it is proposed that the bimetallic MnO2@PtNPs nanoparticles exist in the form of a core-shell structure.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS survey spectra of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, as well as the high-resolution N 1 s core-level spectra of 1-GQDs (b) and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs (c). The XPS survey spectrum of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs reveals the presence of Pt, C, O, N, Mn, and Zn (Fig. 3a). In Fig. 3b, the N 1 s spectrum of 1-GQDs was deconvoluted into three peaks at 396.0 eV (N-C), 397.4 eV (N-H), and 400.0 eV (N-C˭O). The N-C and N-H peaks are attributed to the presence of GQDs (in 1-GQDs) which contain nitrogen functional groups [54,55]. The peak at 400 eV confirms the presence of the amide bond and hence, successful anchoring of 1 to GQDs [56]. Following the adsorption of MnO2@PtNPs onto 1-GQDs to form 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, in Fig. 3c, the N 1s high resolution core level spectrum was deconvoluted, and four peaks were obtained at 396.3 eV (N-C), 397.6 eV (N − Pt), 397.9 eV (N-H), and 400.1 eV (N-C˭O). Interestingly, N-coordinated metal peak was observed and was assigned to the presence of N-Pt in 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, implying exposed Pt sites in the MnO2@PtNPs. It was noted that the position of the amine nitrogen (N-H) was found to be slightly shifted to higher binding energies in the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. A large decrease in the intensity of the amine nitrogen (N-H) was also observed in 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. Both observations imply involvement of amine nitrogen in the interaction with Pt. Shifts to higher binding energies in the nitrogen species can be attributed to the presence of multiple valence electrons in the GQDs which act as electron donors facilitating metal-nitrogen binding interactions [57,58].

Moreover, the high-resolution Pt 4f and Mn 2p core-level spectra of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, are shown in Fig. 3 (d and e). The core-level Pt 4f deconvoluted spectrum of the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs contained two main peaks at 72.4 and 69.3 eV, characteristic peaks of 4f5/2 and 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 3 d). The Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of the MnO2@PtNPs prior to adsorption onto the 1-GQDs were found to be centered at higher binding energies (at 74.17 and 70.88, respectively) compared to those in the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. Shifts to lower binding energies suggest a strong electronic interaction between MnO2@PtNPs and 1-GQDs. The core-level Mn 2p deconvoluted spectrum of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs also revealed two peaks at 639.75 eV and 652.5 eV, attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks, respectively (Fig. 3 e). The Mn2+ satellite peak that was observed in the Mn 2p deconvoluted spectrum of MnO2@PtNPs was absent in 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. The disappearance of this Mn2+ suggested strong electro-electron interaction between the MnO2@PtNPs and 1-GQDs, though a peak attributable to the N-Mn interaction was not observed. The above results suggest strong interaction between MnO2@PtNPs and the 1-GQDs, and taken together, indicate the successful synthesis of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs.

The tumour microenvironment is acidulated and usually characterised by high concentrations of H2O2 [7,8] . The production of O2 can be beneficial for improving tumour oxygen levels as well as regulation of the local pH in tumour tissue, subsequently attenuating the tumour hypoxia microenvironment [4]. Consequently, the ability of MnO2@PtNPs to catalyze H2O2 to O2 was compared to that of MnO2 alone or PtNPs alone. This was assessed using a degassed (Ar saturated) H2O2 solution (5 × 10−3 M) in acetate buffer (pH 5). An oxygen probe (JPBJ-608 Portable Dissolved Oxygen Meter) was used to record the dissolved O2 in H2O2 solutions ( Fig. 4 A). Our results showed that all three samples (MnO2, PtNPs, and MnO2@PtNPs) were able to catalyze H2O2 with subsequent generation of oxygen (Fig. 4 A). For the three samples probed, oxygen generation was fast (bubbles were observed due to oxygen being generated from the solution). Notably, the amount of dissolved oxygen produced in the solution containing MnO2@PtNPs was found to be significantly higher than what was observed for either MnO2 or PtNPs, Fig. 4 A. The breakdown of H2O2 using MnO2@PtNPs was also more rapid, seen from the slope signifying fast oxygen generation (Fig. 4 A). Indeed, these results highlight the synergistic advantages realizable by compositing MnO2 with PtNPs as catalase mimics. In contrast, negligible responses were observed for control samples with only H2O2 over the same time period. The as-prepared MnO2@PtNPs, therefore, showed superior catalytic behaviour towards the catalytic conversion of H2O2 to O2 and was hence employed for PDT experiments.

Electron spin resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was used to elucidate the mechanism involved in the catalysis of H2O2 - either electron transfer or generation of radicals. Fenton’s reaction usually results in the production of reactive oxidative species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals. On the other hand, catalase-like electron transfer processes do not result in the production of hydroxyl radicals. To assess whether the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs was indeed catalase-mimicking, EPR experiments were carried out in the presence of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), a hydroxyl radical scavenger, to investigate the possible production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). DMPO was used to capture short-lived free radicals. DMPO reacts with •OH radicals to generate an active DMPO/•OH adduct with characteristic four well-resolved hyperfine splittings with an intensity of 1:2:2:1 [59]. As can be observed in Fig. 4B, the addition of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs did not result in the generation of •OH radicals. What was seen instead was the presence of six well-resolved hyperfine splitting characteristic of manganese. Mn4+ ions possess paramagnetic nature at either low or high spin. Therefore, EPR experiments only substantiated the presence of MnO2 in the composites. The above results suggest that the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs readily catalyze H2O2 through an electron transfer process and are, therefore, catalase-mimicking.

To further verify the catalase-activity of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs towards H2O2 catalysis, a glassy carbon (GC) electrode was modified (using drop dry method, see Supporting information for details) with 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. Subsequently, cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were taken in the presence and absence of H2O2 in acetate buffer (pH 5.0). The CV of the bare GCE electrode is like that of GCE-1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs within the studied potential window (−1.03 to −0.03 V), Fig. 4C. The GCE did not show an increase in the reduction current both in the absence and presence of H2O2. However, as can be seen in Fig. 4C, the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs modified GC electrode showed an electrocatalytic reduction current following the addition of H2O2, highlighting its ability to catalytically breakdown H2O2. Taken together, the above results suggest that the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs readily catalyze H2O2 through an electron transfer process and are, therefore, catalase-mimicking.

Owing to the limited water solubility of the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, they were subjected to liposome loading. Liposomes are known to load hydrophobic molecules efficiently. Therefore, liposomes can effectively facilitate the delivery of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs into cancer cells. The transmission electron microscopy reveals liposomes with nearly spherical morphologies with a size range of about 150–200 nm ( Fig. 5 ,
Table 2) and are therefore favourable size ranges for PDT, since particles smaller than 10 nm are usually susceptible to renal clearance, while larger particles (greater than 500 nm) are typically subject to hepatic clearance [60]. Therefore, the employed liposomal formulations would be effectively delivered through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect by taking advantage of the leaky vasculature that characterises most tumours. The qualitative elemental mapping of the resultant liposomal nanocomposites was determined using Scanning electron and X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopies (SEM-EDS). Fig. 5 presents the SEM image and the corresponding element mapping of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (as an example). The elements – O, C, Zn, Mn, and Pt, are from 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, while P originates from the DOPC lipid. The coexistence of these elements (distributed within the composites) implies successful liposomal incorporation of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. Additionally, the results obtained were consistent with the expected elemental composition for the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (confirmed by EDS spectral analysis) Fig. 5.

The characteristic absorbance peak attributable to the Q-band of 1, further confirmed the successful encapsulation of hydrophobic 1 or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs into DOPC liposomes, Fig. 6. The improvement in the hydrophilicity of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs is a consequence of liposomal encapsulation. 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, being hydrophobic, is effectively and preferentially incorporated into the lipid bilayer of liposomes [61]. Liposomal formulations presented the typical fluorescence assigned to 1 when excited at the corresponding wavelength (figure not shown). Moreover, the weak fluorescence of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes suggests that the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs is embedded in the lipid bilayers (figure not shown). In each case, the encapsulation efficiency (EE) was determined using equation S1 (Supporting Information). The final drug amount was determined by subtracting the unencapsulated drug (precipitate) weight from the initial drug weight. The encapsulation efficiencies of either 1 or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs in the liposomes were determined to be 48% and 33%, respectively, showing less encapsulation efficiency for the latter, which is larger.

Physicochemical properties of liposomes, such as their size, shape, and charge, are vital parameters in the delivery and improved bio-distribution and prolonged pharmacokinetics of encapsulated cytotoxic drugs. The hydrodynamic diameters of the synthesised liposomes were measured directly after liposome loading by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano ZS. For analysis of the data, the refractive index (1.33) of water at 25 °C was considered. 1-liposomes and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes had sizes of 197.8 nm and 277.5 nm with polydispersity indices (PDI) of 0.271 and 0.470, respectively. Moreover, the formed liposomes were found to be stable with almost unchanged hydrodynamic sizes even after storage at 4 °C for three weeks in the dark (Table S1). DOPC is known to form stable liposomal formulations [62].

Zeta potential (ζ) values were also assessed to determine their surface charges as well as colloidal stability of synthesized liposomal suspensions. All liposomal formulations were found to possess an outer negative charge (Table 2). For 1-liposomes and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, the measured ζ values were − 29.1 ( ± 1.06) mV and –44.2 ( ± 1.97) mV, respectively. As has been previously observed, the negative surface charges on the employed Zwitterion DOPC liposomes in solution may signify that at a molecular level, the phosphate groups are more exposed than the choline moieties on the outer surface of the liposomes [63]. The charge of the phospholipid could affect the pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetic properties of liposomes, and negatively charged liposomal formulations are usually preferred owing to the cytotoxicity associated with their positive counterparts [64–67]. Moreover, because the ζ potential affects the aggregation propensity of a suspension, the high ζ values of the synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes point to appreciable colloidal stabilities (Table 2). The ζ values are lower when 1 and its conjugate are not encapsulated in liposomes, showing less stability.

Uncontrolled (burst) release of drugs from the interior or surface of nanocarriers is a significant concern in drug-delivery systems (DDS) [68]. Burst release of drug cargos can result in non-specific bio-distribution of loaded drugs. To this end, we probed the feasibility of the light-triggered release of employed liposomes. The light-triggered release is of interest since it offers both spatial and temporal control compared to other triggers of targeted release. The loading of PSs into the bilayers of liposomes has been shown to effectively permit the light-triggered release of a huge variety of drug cargos [69,70]. In our case, this was assessed by observing the change in the fluorescence spectra of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes following light irradiation. Quite simply, since 1 is reasonably fluorescent, its fluorescence within the liposome could be monitored following light irradiation. The results show a gradual reduction in the fluorescence of a diluted sample of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (Fig. S6). Effectively, irradiation leads to the lysing of the liposome by lipid peroxidation, and because this leads to the release of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, its fluorescence would gradually decrease as more 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs is released from the liposome. The results are not unexpected considering that the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs are located within the bilayer of the liposomes and, as such, can induce damage to the liposome. The 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs once released is not water soluble, hence the only fluorescence is due the remaining encapsulated conjugate. Similar light-triggered liposome responses have been reported using trisulfonated aluminum phthalocyanine (AlPcS3) [71]. The final solution was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy and showed the presence of lysed liposomes (Fig. S6 (ii)) when compared to before irradiation, Fig. S6 (i). Our results showed that for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, NIR irradiation was enough to effect appreciable liposomal lysing (Fig. S6). The NIR-triggered release of the employed liposomes is represented by Fig. S7. This proceeds through lipid peroxidation by generated singlet oxygen by 1, a constituent of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (Fig. S7). Light-induced peroxidation by oxygen is suggested to occur in the two acyl chains of DOPC since they contain double bonds, resulting in membrane permeabilization [72]. This inevitably leads to the formation of pores in the bilayer, which causes drug/cargo leakage [69]. Our results are in line with a body of literature, showing that DOPC systems readily accelerate light-triggered release in liposomal formulations [72–74]. Light as a release trigger holds promise over many other triggers since it offers control over parameters such as exposure time and can be externally modified to adapt to different purposes.

Descriptions of equations used in this work can be found in the Supporting Information. The photophysicochemical properties of the 1 and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs are summarized in Table 1. There was an observed decrease in the fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs, compared to 1 alone or 1-GQDs, Table 1. This marked decrease in fluorescence quantum yields for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs may be attributed to the heavy atom effects of metallic nanoparticles (such as Pt constituted in MnO2@PtNPs), which may encourage intersystem crossing to the triplet states of MPcs. However, this decrease in fluorescence was accompanied by a corresponding increase in the fluorescence lifetime (τF) for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs. It has been reported that the radiative decay rate of fluorophore can be increased or decreased by metallic nanoparticles depending on the orientation between the molecule and the nanoparticles [75]. Increased singlet state lifetimes are desirable since a longer-lived singlet state may translate into high singlet oxygen generations. This is because a higher singlet state lifetime means that the molecule stays longer in the first excited singlet state, S1. Consequently, there is a higher probability of intersystem crossing to populate the triplet state from which singlet oxygen is generated. Moreover, a decrease in fluorescence parameters may be ascribed to quenching between GQDs and respective Pcs due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and other factors that influence the reduction in GQDs emission in addition to FRET [76]. The fluorescence parameters for GQDs alone have been reported before [77].

Furthermore, electron spin resonance (EPR) spin-trapping technique was used to assess ROS generation induced by 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used to probe the existence of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (O2•−) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used to probe the generation of singlet oxygen (1O2). Fig. S9 shows the electron spin resonance spectra of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. No apparent signals attributable to either DMPO/•OH and DMPO/O2•− radical adducts were observed (Fig. S9A), which indicated that neither hydroxyl radicals nor superoxide radicals are generated by the employed 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. In the presence of singlet oxygen, TEMP is oxidized to 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPO), which is observed as three hyperfine splitting signals of equal intensities [78]. As shown in Fig. S9B, the ESR signal of TEMPO was observed, suggesting the production of singlet oxygen by 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. In this way, the generation of singlet oxygen under light irradiation indicates that 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes can effectively generate singlet oxygen in aqueous environments, making it applicable for use in PDT. The therapeutic efficacy of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes for PDT was then assessed under both normoxia and hypoxia.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CoCl2 on MCF-7 cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, CoCl2 was used as a hypoxia-mimicking agent. CoCl2 strongly stabilizes the hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF-1α) - the central signaling factor governing hypoxia [79]. Moreover, with CoCl2 treatment, the stabilization of HIF-1α is sustained for several hours, allowing a wider time window during which samples can be manipulated and analyzed, a feature often necessary for in vitro PDT. Consequently, CoCl2 has been widely used as a hypoxia-mimicking agent in both in vivo [80] and in vitro studies [79]. To select the experimental concentration of CoCl2, a 24 h dose-response study was conducted by exposing cells to different concentrations of CoCl2 (50–200 µM). The results revealed that there was no significant difference in cell viability between the cells treated with 50, 100, and 150 µM of CoCl2. However, beyond these concentrations, cell viability was reduced (Fig. S10). The above result is in line with numerous studies that have demonstrated that a CoCl2 concentration of 150 µM is effective in inducing hypoxia in MCF-7 cells without cytotoxicity over a 24 h period [81,82]. In subsequent studies, the concentrations of CoCl2 was set at 150 µM. For all concentrations, no morphological changes were observed post CoCl2 treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake under normoxia and hypoxia
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro experiments were conducted to determine the PDT effectiveness of the as synthesized nanoconjugates. Firstly, their cellular uptake efficiencies were evaluated. Differential drug uptake features under both normoxia and hypoxia are key considerations in cancer drug designs. The energy reliance of endocytic uptake of nanomedicines means that these hypoxia-induced changes have the potential to undermine their uptake and internalization propensities. The relatively short half-life and low diffusion distances of singlet oxygen further necessitated the evaluation of the uptake of the composites employed in this work into the employed MCF-7 cells. The internalization of the respective liposome-loaded composites was assessed within the cells. Generally, a time-dependent intracellular drug uptake was observed ( Fig. 7). 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes drug uptake was noticeably higher than for 1-liposome, further demonstrating the latter’s applicability in PDT.

To evaluate the feasibility of the composites to be employed as PDT agents, their cellular dark cytotoxicity toward human breast adenocarcinoma cells (MCF-7) were assessed under both normoxia and CoCl2-induced hypoxia. This was achieved using typical colorimetric cell viability WST experiments [83], with or without laser irradiation. Because not all the drugs (1-liposomes or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes) are taken in by the MCF-7 cells, residual drug materials in media under NIR irradiation could also generate singlet oxygen leading to tumour cell death. To rule this out, the media was replaced with fresh media in each case to ensure the cytotoxicity was attributed to either 1-liposomes or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes accumulated in the tumour cells.

As shown in Fig. S11A, negligible toxicity was observed for all the formulations without laser irradiation representing low dark cytotoxicity of the liposomal-loaded drugs. As no laser was applied, no singlet oxygen should be produced, and hence low cytotoxicity should be expected. Moreover, since the release mechanism of the employed DOPC liposomes occurs through irradiation, no cytotoxicity was anticipated in the dark. Therefore, the employed nanocomposites do not induce an apparent adverse effect on the cellular viabilities within 24 h of incubation at a concentration range used. These results suggest good biocompatibility of the nanocomposites for therapeutic applications, a feature resulting from liposome encapsulation. Cell death was only observed following light irradiation in the presence of either 1-liposomes or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, Fig. S11B. Cell death attributable to the heating effect of NIR irradiation was ruled out using appropriate controls. Treatment with 1-liposomes resulted in a 40% reduction in cell viability under NIR light irradiation at the highest concentration of at 100 µg/mL. Comparatively, after the treatment with 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes under NIR light irradiation, around 60% of tumour cells were killed. Therefore, improved PDT lethality and hence lower cell viabilities were noted for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes in a concentration-dependent manner.

The intracellular ROS level achieved using either 1-liposomes and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes was assessed by DCFH-DA assay [84]. The ROS probe 2,7-dichloriflurescin diacetate (DCFH-DA assay) was used for semi-quantitative analysis. DCFH-DA is cell-permeable and non-fluorescent. Upon uptake by the cell, DCFH-DA is acted upon by intracellular esterases to cleave the acetate groups. Following this, ROS oxidation leads to its conversion to the highly fluorescent 2',7'-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Therefore, the degree of DCF fluorescence can be used as a measure of intracellular ROS. A microplate analyzer was used to measure DCF fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 485 nm and emission of 535 nm. Control experiments were performed with no drug present (DCFH-DA only), and H2O2 served as a positive control. When compared to the dark controls, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed for cells treated with either 1-liposomes or 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes when photoactivated (Fig. S12). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S12, a dramatically enhanced fluorescence was present within cells treated with 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. A more moderate DCF fluorescence was observed for cells treated with 1-liposomes. The enhanced DCF fluorescence observed for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes treated cells signifies significantly higher intracellular ROS production. The higher ROS response for 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes is consistent with the higher PDT activity observed (Fig. S11).

Hypoxia conditioning referenced in this work was achieved by incubating cells in CoCl2 for a period of 24 h. It is important to note that besides CoCl2 pre-treatment, hypoxia in this work is also potentiated by PDT treatment through the consumption of oxygen by the PS. As can be observed in Fig. 8A,C, low dark cytotoxicity was elucidated from the liposome-loaded composites (for both 1-liposomes and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes) with the cells subjected to hypoxic conditioning remaining viable even at the highest concentration of 100 µg/mL. We observed that following irradiation, 1-liposomes was unable to induce considerable PDT effect on the cells exposed to CoCl2 pre-conditioning. At a maximal drug concentration of 100 ug/mL, only 10% cell death was observed for 1-liposomes (Fig. 8B). Previous reports have indeed shown that CoCl2 induced hypoxia promotes cancer cell resistance to PDT [85–88]. Moreover, oxygen-dependent PDT greatly potentiates hypoxia within the cells. As a result, 1-liposomes is unable to effect cell death owing to the low intracellular oxygen amount in vitro following light irradiation since it consumes oxygen to generate singlet oxygen.

Our results, however, show that 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes remained effective under hypoxia, effecting significant photodynamic lethality following light irradiation (Fig. 8B). Furthermore, the phototoxicity increased along with the concentration of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. Intriguingly, the photo-induced toxicity of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes under hypoxia (50% cell death) was comparable to what was observed in normoxia (about 60% cell death). The observed phototoxicity of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes was attributed to its ability to supplement intracellular oxygen, a feature not shared by 1-liposomes. It is important to note that, even at the employed irradiance of 0.28 W cm-2, the employed 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes could enact photodynamic lethality to the employed MCF-7 cancer cells. Upon irradiation, PSs produce singlet oxygen form ground-state tissue oxygen. This photochemical process (production of singlet oxygen from ground-state tissue oxygen) is irradiance and fluence rate dependent, with higher irradiances resulting in higher oxygen consumption [17,89]. In essence, owing to the ability of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes to generate O2 from H2O2 break down, a continuous generation of oxygen from hydrogen peroxide and eventual conversion to singlet oxygen is realized. This observation explains the higher PDT activity of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes (Fig. 8B), even within cells subjected to hypoxic preconditioning and highlights its potential in hypoxic PDT.

Morphological characteristics of MCF-7 cells were also assessed following treatment with CoCl2 alone, CoCl2 with laser irradiation, and PDT (treated with CoCl2 and 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes and irradiated). CoCl2 treatment (both with and without irradiation, figure not shown) had no detectable influence on the morphology of the cells. However, post-PDT treatment with 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, the cells changed from a characteristic epithelial-like appearance and became irregular, with some cells rounding off, implying cell death (Fig. 8).

To further probe the merits of the employed formulations, exogenous H2O2 was introduced into the cell environment. It is known that cancer cells inside solid tumours can constitutively produce H2O2, which may, in turn, enhance neoplastic behaviours of tumour cells·H2O2 is an abundant tumour metabolite (present at concentrations ranging from 100 μM to 1 mM) [90,91]. To this end, MCF-7 cells were subjected to hypoxic pre-conditioning followed by incubation with exogenous H2O2 (50 µM). These cells were then irradiated with an NIR light for 10 min at the power density of 280 mW cm−2. After incubation for another 24 h with a fresh cell medium, the cell viability assay was used to determine the PDT efficacy.

Under light irradiation, we found a dramatically enhanced PDT efficacy for the MnO2@PtNPs constituted 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. The observed cell viabilities of the MCF-7 cells subjected to 1-liposome treatment were relatively unchanged, with only about 15% cell death (Fig. 8D). The higher cell viability observed in 1-liposomes loaded cells may have been because of insufficient oxygen to produce singlet oxygen through the photodynamic effect. The higher photodynamic efficacy of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes may then be mainly ascribed to their O2 self-generating ability through H2O2 catalysis. This may be credited to the presence of the MnO2@PtNPs in 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes. Therefore, 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes could trigger cell apoptosis through a ROS-mediated mechanism and serve as H2O2-responsive photodynamic agents.

The enhanced PDT effect of 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes is attributed to its ability to supplement intracellular oxygen levels in MCF-7 cancer cells, which facilitated hypoxic tumour cell ablation when combined with subsequent NIR irradiation.

Here, the successful synthesis of a novel multifunctional phthalocyanine-nanoparticle hybrid system, 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes, is presented. We herein demonstrate the superior catalase-mimicking properties of MnO2@PtNPs over MnO2 or PtNPs alone. We show that by combining each function of the respective building blocks, the as-synthesized 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes not only maintains the properties of oxygen supplementation through H2O2 catalysis but also displayed cooperative properties for enhanced singlet oxygen production. Such 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes show efficient passive tumour cell accumulation. Our results indicate that the 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes showed no dark toxicity but exhibited lethality post laser irradiation in both normoxia and hypoxia, therefore, demonstrating enhanced cancer therapy, a feature attributed to its ability to supplement oxygen in vitro. Thus, 1-GQDs-MnO2@PtNPs-liposomes shows great promise for cancer treatment via PDT-induced therapy. This work further lays credence to the importance of hypoxia in photodynamic therapy.

Nnamdi Nwahara: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Muthumuni Managa: Characterization of materials. Jonathan Britton: Characterization of materials. Mihlali Stoffels: Provided facilities and assisted with biological studies. Earl Prinsloo: Provided facilities and assisted with biological studies. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, reviewing, editing, funding acquisition.
